summary: Stancil argues that data teams over-invest in validating inputs and lineage, yet trust in metrics actually comes from output consistency over time. Because users judge credibility by whether numbers stay stable across reports, he proposes tools and practices that surface changes and drift in outputs rather than just monitoring pipelines and tests.
money_quotes: No, everyone else uses Method 2: “Do I believe this number, given what I believed yesterday?”
In other words, trust isn’t built by confirming our sources; it’s built via mathematical induction.
To trust the output, test the output.
The point is to reframe the problem around validating outputs instead of inputs.
Because in data, there are only two possible constants: Consistent metrics or consistent questions.
key_insight: Trust in metrics comes less from perfect pipelines and more from visibly consistent outputs over time, so data tooling should highlight change and drift in results.
